summary:
Stoney Littleton Long Barrow is a Neolithic chambered long barrow located near Wellow in Somerset, England, dating to approximately 4000–3500 BCE. It is one of the best-preserved megalithic gallery graves in the United Kingdom and an example of the Severn-Cotswold chambered tomb tradition of southwestern England. The monument consists of a trapezoidal earthen mound approximately 30 meters long containing a stone-lined entrance passage leading to three pairs of side chambers and a terminal chamber, collectively capable of holding the remains of many individuals deposited over successive generations.

Like most megalithic long barrows of the British Neolithic, Stoney Littleton was a communal tomb — a house for the ancestral dead — where the bones of community members were placed over an extended period. The chambers likely served as a focal point for ritual activity, ancestor veneration, and community identity. The entrance is oriented toward the rising sun around the winter solstice, a common feature of Neolithic monuments reflecting the cosmological importance of solar cycles to farming communities. The monument was partially restored in the nineteenth century after deterioration.

Stoney Littleton represents the [[event:35|megalithic cultural tradition]] of western Britain, connected to the broader Atlantic façade tradition of chambered tomb building and expressing the characteristic Neolithic investment in ancestral monumentality. It is a scheduled ancient monument protected as part of Britain's prehistoric heritage.
